era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, a pivotal year marking the formal establishment of the Second Athenian League and the beginning of a major realignment of Greek political power. Sparta's hegemony is being challenged; Thebes is rising; Athens is recovering from the Peloponnesian War. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II remains the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean.

events.title: Founding of the Second Athenian League
events.description: Athens formally establishes the Second Athenian League (also called the Second Athenian Confederacy), a military and political alliance of Greek city-states designed to counterbalance Spartan hegemony. The League includes numerous Aegean island states and coastal cities. Unlike the earlier Delian League (which became an Athenian empire), the Second Athenian League is theoretically based on the principle of equality among members, with Athens as the leading power. This represents a major reassertion of Athenian power and influence in the Greek world following the Peloponnesian War and the oligarchic period.

sources.name: Inscriptions recording League charter and decrees (IG II²)

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Second Athenian League in 378 BCE is well-attested in both literary sources and contemporary inscriptions. This is one of the most securely dated events of this period.

events.title: Spartan aggression against Thebes and Athens provokes anti-Spartan coalition
events.description: Sparta, under King Agesilaus II, continues aggressive military campaigns to maintain its hegemony over Greece. Spartan actions against Thebes and Athens provoke a coalition of Greek states to form against Spartan dominance. The founding of the Second Athenian League is a direct response to Spartan aggression. This marks the beginning of the end of Spartan hegemony in Greece, as other powers unite against Spartan military dominance.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Spartan aggression and the anti-Spartan response is well-attested; specific military actions of 378 BCE are not precisely dated.

events.title: Plato's Academy continues as center of philosophical inquiry
events.description: Plato's Academy, founded c. 387 BCE, continues to operate as the premier philosophical institution in the Greek world. By 378 BCE, Plato is in his late 40s and is actively engaged in philosophical inquiry and teaching. The Academy attracts students from across the Greek world and represents a new model of sustained intellectual community. This institution will profoundly influence Western philosophy for centuries.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The existence and operation of Plato's Academy in this period is well-attested.

events.title: Dionysius I of Syracuse continues military campaigns in Sicily
events.description: Dionysius I (r. 405–367 BCE) continues his military campaigns to consolidate control over Sicily and to contest Carthaginian power on the island. By 378 BCE, Dionysius has established a powerful military state with advanced siege machinery and military innovations. His rule represents a new model of Hellenistic tyranny that will influence later political developments.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Dionysius I is well-documented in later sources; specific military campaigns of 378 BCE are not precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise date of the founding of the Second Athenian League is sometimes given as 377 BCE rather than 378 BCE in different sources. Modern scholars debate the exact structure and membership of the League, with some questioning the degree of Athenian dominance and the extent to which the League was truly based on equality. The significance attributed to the League in later historiography may reflect Athenian bias in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 378 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon and later compilations by Diodorus and Plutarch. Epigraphic evidence (inscriptions) provides contemporary documentation of the League's charter and early decrees. These sources focus on political and military history and provide limited information about economic, social, or cultural developments. The record is heavily biased toward the Greek mainland; information about the Persian Empire, Egypt, and other regions is minimal. Archaeological evidence for this specific year is not available.
